title: William (hippopotamus figurine)

infobox.value: [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States")

is an [Egyptian faience](/wiki/Egyptian_faience "Egyptian faience") hippopotamus statuette from the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt"), now in the collection of the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), where it serves as an informal mascot for the museum. Found in a shaft associated with the [Upper Egyptian](/wiki/Upper_Egypt "Upper Egypt") tomb chapel of "The Steward, Senbi", in what is now [Meir](/wiki/Meir,_Egypt "Meir, Egypt"), William dates from c. 1961 BC – c. 1878 BC, during the reigns of [Senusret I](/wiki/Senusret_I "Senusret I") and [Senusret II](/wiki/Senusret_II "Senusret II").[[1]](#cite_note-metmuseum1-1) This 20 cm (8 in) figurine in Egyptian faience, a [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay")-less material, has become popular not only for his endearing appearance, but also because his defining characteristics illustrate many of the most salient facets of [craft production in ancient Egypt](/wiki/Art_of_ancient_Egypt "Art of ancient Egypt") during this time.

David Frederick Grose, *The Toledo Museum of Art, Early Ancient Glass: Core-Formed, Rod-Formed, and Cast Vessels and Objects from the Late Bronze Age to the Early Roman Empire, 1600 BC to AD 50* (Manchester: Hudson Hills Press, 1999), 29.
Paul T. Nicholson, *Egyptian Faience and Glass* (Buckinghamshire: Shire, 1993), 45.

["William and his Friends, A Group of Notable Creatures in The Metropolitan Museum of Art"](http://designarchives.aiga.org/#/entries/%2Bid%3A18968/_/detail/relevance/asc/0/3/18968/william-and-his-friends-a-group-of-notable-creatures-in-the-metropolitan-museum-of-art/1). *American Institute of Graphic Arts*.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Egyptian hippopotamus figurine

| William | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Material | [Egyptian faience](/wiki/Egyptian_faience "Egyptian faience") |
| Length | 20 cm |
| Height | 11.2 cm |
| Width | 7.5 cm |
| Created | c. 1920 BC |
| Present location | [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") |
| Identification | [17.9.1](https://www.metmuseum.org/art/collection/search/544227) |

**William** is an [Egyptian faience](/wiki/Egyptian_faience "Egyptian faience") hippopotamus statuette from the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt"), now in the collection of the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), where it serves as an informal mascot for the museum. Found in a shaft associated with the [Upper Egyptian](/wiki/Upper_Egypt "Upper Egypt") tomb chapel of "The Steward, Senbi", in what is now [Meir](/wiki/Meir,_Egypt "Meir, Egypt"), William dates from c. 1961 BC – c. 1878 BC, during the reigns of [Senusret I](/wiki/Senusret_I "Senusret I") and [Senusret II](/wiki/Senusret_II "Senusret II").[[1]](#cite_note-metmuseum1-1) This 20 cm (8 in) figurine in Egyptian faience, a [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay")-less material, has become popular not only for his endearing appearance, but also because his defining characteristics illustrate many of the most salient facets of [craft production in ancient Egypt](/wiki/Art_of_ancient_Egypt "Art of ancient Egypt") during this time.

William is only one of several objects associated with the tomb of "The Steward, Senbi", which were acquired by the Metropolitan Museum in 1917. According to the Museum's *Bulletin* from that year, this hippopotamus is a "particularly fine example of a type found, in common with various other animal forms, among the funerary furnishings of tombs of the Middle Kingdom" and also an exemplary piece of Egyptian faience.[[2]](#cite_note-Egyptian_Hippopotamus_Figure_1917-2) William may be seen in Gallery 111 at the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art") in New York City.

## Egyptian faience

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_(hippopotamus_figurine)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Egyptian faience")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Statuette_of_Isis_and_Horus_MET_DP241036.jpg/120px-Statuette_of_Isis_and_Horus_MET_DP241036.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statuette_of_Isis_and_Horus_MET_DP241036.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Bowl_MET_DP243440_%28cropped%29.jpg/120px-Bowl_MET_DP243440_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bowl_MET_DP243440_(cropped).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Sphinx_of_Amenhotep_III%2C_possibly_from_a_Model_of_a_Temple_MET_DT539.jpg/250px-Sphinx_of_Amenhotep_III%2C_possibly_from_a_Model_of_a_Temple_MET_DT539.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sphinx_of_Amenhotep_III,_possibly_from_a_Model_of_a_Temple_MET_DT539.jpg)

Examples of Egyptian faience artworks in the Metropolitan Museum

Related to glass insofar as it attains a "vitreous" appearance, [Egyptian faience](/wiki/Egyptian_faience "Egyptian faience") was, according to [David Grose](/wiki/David_Grose "David Grose"), "a material made from powdered quartz covered with a true vitreous coating." Significantly more porous and malleable than glass proper, faience could be shaped by hand or cast in molds to create vessels or other objects.[[3]](#cite_note-3) While similar raw materials are involved in the creation of both faience and glass (crushed quartz or sand mixed with lime and either [natron](/wiki/Natron "Natron") or plant ash), faience ultimately lacks the rigid crystalline structure of modern glass.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Significantly, both glass and faience were used, according to Paul T. Nicholson, to imitate semi-precious stones in less costly materials, and both were valued for their beauty and durability.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Although not [pottery](/wiki/Pottery "Pottery") on a strict definition, since it contains no [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay"), faience is often treated as pottery.

This statuette demonstrates the importance of a variety of iconographic styles in Middle Kingdom era burial practices. As Nicholson writes, animal figures were common during this period and "hippopotamus figurines, usually decorated with aquatic plants, probably symbolized the revitalizing properties of the Nile". They may have held some kind of religious significance, as they were sometimes associated with one of the forms of [Seth](/wiki/Set_(mythology) "Set (mythology)").[[5]](#cite_note-5) As not all hippopotamus statues from this era were elaborately painted, William is a particularly important example; he has been "covered with a decoration in black line of lotus flowers, buds, and leaves" to signal his "natural surroundings among the lowlands of the Nile."[[2]](#cite_note-Egyptian_Hippopotamus_Figure_1917-2) As noted in the Metropolitan Museum's summary, the [hippopotamus](/wiki/Hippopotamus "Hippopotamus") was one of the most threatening animals for ancient Egyptians and, in this case, three of William's legs probably were broken purposely to prevent him from harming the deceased in the afterlife (the museum restored these legs).[[1]](#cite_note-metmuseum1-1)

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_(hippopotamus_figurine)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Name")]

In the early 20th century, Captain H. M. Raleigh and his family owned a photograph of the hippopotamus, and began to refer to him as William. Raleigh published an article about the hippo for the magazine *[Punch](/wiki/Punch_(magazine) "Punch (magazine)")* on 18 March 1931, in which he wrote, "He is described on the back of the frame as "Hippopotamus with Lotus Flowers, Buds and Leaves, XII Dynasty (about 1950 BC), Series VII, Number i, Egyptian Faience;" but to us he is simply William."[[6]](#cite_note-WilliamName-6)[[7]](#cite_note-NY_Times:_FYI-7) The article was reprinted in the Metropolitan Museum of Art's *Bulletin* in June 1931, and the name stuck.[[6]](#cite_note-WilliamName-6) In 1936, the Met released a book entitled, *William and his Friends: A Group of Notable Creatures in the Metropolitan Museum of Art.*[[8]](#cite_note-William_and_His_Friends,_1936-8) Since then, William has continued to appear in some museum logos and merchandise for both children and adults. The Met first began selling cast reproductions of William in the 1950s,[[7]](#cite_note-NY_Times:_FYI-7) which today are manufactured by M. Hart Pottery.[[9]](#cite_note-9)
